content.text: When a Cowork task starts, the agentic engine does not read only your goal statement. It assembles a layered context window that combines persistent project knowledge, the task goal, live workspace files, and connector-retrieved data. Understanding how this assembly works lets you write memory and goals that reliably produce the output you want.
What Context Means to the Cowork Engine

attrs.alt: Context Hierarchy in Cowork: four stacked layers from Project Memory to Connector Query Results

content.text: The Cowork engine is a large language model running inside an automated execution loop. At task start it is handed a single context window — one continuous block of tokens — that contains everything it needs. There is no dynamic retrieval during reasoning (only connector tool calls); once the task begins, the context is fixed. Every decision the engine makes flows from what it read at that moment.

The four layers are injected in order: project memory first, task goal second, workspace file contents third, connector query results last. This order reflects priority: earlier content provides stable background; later content provides fresh, specific data that can narrow or override earlier signals.

Project memory is a free-text block you write when setting up the project. It is injected verbatim at the start of every task run in the project. It should contain the information that stays constant across all tasks: output format conventions, audience notes, recurring data source paths, domain vocabulary, and team preferences.

## Project: Q2 Revenue Analysis
Output format: markdown with a summary table at the top
Audience: non-technical finance stakeholders (avoid jargon)
Data location: /workspace/revenue/ (CSV files, updated monthly)
Currency: USD; use comma-separated thousands (e.g. 1,200,000)
Do not include raw query results in outputs — summarize them
Memory is written once and shared across runs. Avoid putting task-specific details here — that is the job of the task goal.

The task goal is the instruction you write for a single run. It inherits everything in project memory automatically — you do not restate conventions, data paths, or format rules unless this task deviates from them. A well-scoped goal is short and specific.
Summarize Q2 regional sales by territory using /workspace/revenue/q2_by_region.csv. Highlight the top 3 and bottom 3 territories by growth rate. Flag any territory with negative YoY growth.

If the goal contradicts memory (for example, memory says output in markdown but the goal says output as a CSV), the goal wins. Use this intentionally when a single task needs a different format without permanently changing the project convention.

Files in the project workspace (/workspace/) are read fresh at every task run. The engine scans the directory and makes file contents available to the context. This is the primary mechanism for bringing in live data without updating memory: upload a new CSV before each run and the engine reads the latest version automatically.

/workspace/
  revenue/
    q2_by_region.csv      <- updated before each run
    q1_by_region.csv      <- retained for YoY comparison
  templates/
    report_template.md    <- stable; engine reads as format guide
  outputs/
    q1_summary.md         <- prior run output; engine may reference it
Large files are truncated to fit the context window. For files over ~50 KB, store only the relevant subset in the workspace and keep raw archives elsewhere.

When a task step calls a connector (Google Drive, Slack, Jira, GitHub), the returned data is appended to context at that step. Connector results are the freshest data in the context — fetched on demand rather than at task start. The scope of what a connector can retrieve is bounded by the OAuth permissions granted when the connector was set up.
Memory is the highest-leverage context input. Poor memory produces inconsistent outputs across runs; well-written memory eliminates the need to repeat the same instructions in every goal.
The engine responds to declarative, specific statements. Concrete constraints outperform vague preferences. Reference exact file paths, format names, and numeric thresholds rather than adjectives.

# DO — specific and actionable
Output: always produce a markdown file with a H2 summary at the top
Numbers: format as comma-separated (1,234,567) with two decimal places
Sources: use /workspace/data/ exclusively; do not fetch external URLs
Scope: analyze only the current quarter unless the goal explicitly says otherwise
Uncertainty: if data is missing for a region, note it explicitly rather than interpolating

# DON'T — vague and unactionable
Be professional
Use good formatting
Make the output clear and readable
Common patterns that degrade memory effectiveness:
Memory is for stable context; task-specific details clutter it and cause stale instructions
Move task-specific scope to the goal
Engine picks one rule arbitrarily when two contradict; output is unpredictable
Remove the weaker rule or qualify each with a condition
Long paragraphs are harder for the engine to extract and apply than a bulleted list
Use short declarative bullets or key: value lines
Engine reads stale paths as authoritative; connector calls or file reads fail silently
Update memory whenever project structure changes
Memory that covers every edge case grows too long and competes with the token budget
Cover the 80% case; use goal overrides for exceptions
The single most common authoring mistake is putting task-specific details in memory or project-wide conventions in the goal. Each layer has a distinct responsibility.
What the Goal Should and Should Not Contain

The goal should contain: the specific action for this run, the input file or connector source for this run, any one-time constraint that differs from memory, and the expected output artifact name. The goal should not contain: format rules (in memory), audience notes (in memory), recurring data paths already listed in memory, or explanations of context the engine already has.

# BAD — goal restates memory
Generate a markdown report (use markdown format with H2 at top, comma-separated numbers).
Audience is finance stakeholders so avoid jargon.
Use /workspace/revenue/ for data.
Analyze Q2 revenue by region and highlight the top 3.

# GOOD — goal only adds new information
Analyze Q2 revenue by region from /workspace/revenue/q2_by_region.csv.
Highlight the top 3 and bottom 3 territories by growth rate.
Save output to /workspace/outputs/q2_regional_report.md

Override is intentional and explicit. If memory says 'output as markdown' and a one-off task needs JSON for a downstream API, state that in the goal. The engine will use JSON for that run and return to markdown on the next.

# Memory says: output as markdown
# This goal overrides for one run:
Output the regional breakdown as a JSON array (not markdown) so it can be
loaded directly into the dashboard API. Schema: [{region, q2_revenue, yoy_growth}]
Narrowing or widening for this run

All four context layers must fit the model context window. The Cowork engine uses a large-context model, but the budget is finite. When total context exceeds the window, earlier content (project memory) is compressed or truncated first, then workspace files. Task goal and recent connector results are prioritized.
Grows with file size and count
When workspace files are large or connector results are verbose, the engine may truncate context. Strategies to stay within budget:
Store only the relevant rows of a large CSV; pre-filter before uploading to /workspace/
Reference connector data by specific query rather than 'read everything'; scope the connector call in the goal
Split a large task into sequential smaller tasks that each consume a smaller context slice
Keep memory under 400 words; verbose memory consumes budget on every single task run
Archive outputs from prior runs outside /workspace/ once they are no longer needed as reference

# Goal pattern for large datasets — pre-filter instruction
Filter /workspace/transactions.csv to rows where region = 'EMEA' and date >= 2024-04-01.
Save the filtered subset to /workspace/emea_q2.csv, then analyze that file for revenue trends.

# This keeps the analysis step's context window focused on a smaller file.

Workspace files are the primary mechanism for injecting fresh data into a stable project without modifying memory. The pattern is to treat memory as immutable during a project run and let the workspace absorb all variability.

A practical workflow: before each task run, upload the latest data file to the project workspace. The engine re-reads the workspace on every run, so the new file is automatically in context. The goal references the file by path, and memory records the convention for where data lives.

# Recurring monthly workflow

# Step 1 (you do this): Upload updated file
# drag-drop revenue_2024-06.csv into /workspace/revenue/

# Step 2 (task goal)
Analyze /workspace/revenue/revenue_2024-06.csv.
Compare to /workspace/revenue/revenue_2024-05.csv for MoM change.
Output summary to /workspace/outputs/2024-06_revenue_summary.md

# Memory stays the same across every month's run — only the goal changes
No — memory is the right layer
No — memory cannot hold file contents
Yes — engine reads them as context
Most context-related failures fall into four patterns. Each has a clear diagnostic signal and a straightforward fix.

Memory that uses adjectives ('professional', 'clear', 'concise') instead of concrete rules gives the engine no reliable signal. The engine fills vague instructions with its own defaults, which may not match your expectations.

# Vague (avoid)
Write professional reports that are easy to understand.
Use appropriate formatting.

# Specific (prefer)
Reports: H2 title, bullet summary (5 bullets max), then detailed sections
Sentence length: max 25 words per sentence in summary section
Technical terms: define on first use in parentheses

When memory says one thing and the goal implies another, the engine must choose. It usually follows the goal, but ambiguous cases produce mixed outputs. Detect this by checking if a task output contradicts a memory rule you expected to apply.

# Memory says:
Output format: CSV with headers, one row per territory

# Goal says:
Summarize the Q2 results with a narrative paragraph for each region.

# Result: engine may output CSV or narrative or both — unpredictable
# Fix: either change memory to allow both formats, or add an explicit override in the goal:
# 'For this run, output as narrative paragraphs (not CSV as specified in memory)'
Memory that grows without pruning accumulates outdated rules, redundant constraints, and low-priority details that crowd out high-priority content. Signs of bloat:
Memory exceeds 600 words — split into project-level and task-level conventions
Memory contains rules that apply to only one past task — move them to that task's goal
Rules in memory contradict each other — prune the older or weaker one
Output quality degrades on simple tasks — the engine may be distracted by irrelevant memory content

If a workspace file is not updated before a task run, the engine reads the old version and produces outdated analysis. For recurring tasks, make file refresh a pre-task ritual: upload the new version, verify the path matches the goal, then run. The engine has no way to detect that a file is stale — that responsibility belongs to you.
Connectors add a dynamic retrieval layer that workspace files cannot provide: they query live external systems at task execution time. The context impact depends on connector type and query scope.

When a task step triggers a connector call (for example, 'retrieve recent Slack messages from #analytics-team'), the results are appended to the active context. For long tasks with multiple connector calls, each result adds to context. Plan connector scope accordingly.

# Goal that scopes connector calls precisely:
Retrieve Slack messages from #analytics-team posted in the last 7 days (connector: Slack).
Filter to messages that mention 'Q2 revenue' or 'forecast'.
Summarize the three most discussed open questions.
Do not retrieve messages from other channels.

# Why: 'last 7 days' and 'this channel only' prevent the connector from
# pulling a large volume of messages that would bloat the context.
List all open issues in the project
List issues labeled 'revenue' updated this sprint
Read all PRs in the repo
Read PRs merged this week in the /analytics path

When a task produces unexpected output, the most likely cause is a context problem: memory the engine did not apply, a workspace file it could not read, or a connector result that contradicted memory. Debug in this order:
Check the steps view for the connector calls made — did the engine query what you expected?
Verify the workspace file exists at the path the goal references — a wrong path means the engine skips the file silently
Read your memory block and the task goal together — look for ambiguities or contradictions
Check the output for evidence of which layer the engine prioritized — does it look like memory was applied? Goal? Connector data?

If needed, run a diagnostic task: a goal that says 'list all files you can see in /workspace/ and summarize the project memory rules you have been given' — this surfaces what the engine actually received

# Diagnostic task goal
Do not perform any analysis. Instead:
1. List all files visible in /workspace/ with their sizes
2. List the project memory rules you have been given, verbatim
3. Confirm which connector(s) are available to you

This output is for debugging — accuracy over brevity.
Context problems are easier to prevent than diagnose. Review memory and workspace contents before starting a new task phase, especially after changing team conventions or data sources.
Context is assembled from four ordered layers: project memory, task goal, workspace files, and connector results.
Project memory is injected at every task start — keep it stable, specific, and under 400 words.
Task goals inherit memory automatically; use them to add task-specific scope, not to restate conventions.
Workspace files provide fresh data on each run; the upload-then-run workflow avoids modifying memory for routine data refreshes.
Connector results are fetched at execution time; scope queries narrowly to manage context budget.
When the total context exceeds the window, the engine truncates workspace files and compresses memory — structure both to remain useful under truncation.
Debug unexpected outputs by verifying connector calls, workspace paths, and memory-goal coherence in that order.
